coredata.dc:title: Multicomponent solvent extraction modelling of lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese from simulated black mass leachate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Junnan
Li, Wen
Stevens, Geoff W.
Mumford, Kathryn A.
coredata.dc:description: The role of lithium-ion batteries in facilitating the global shift towards renewable energy is paramount. Managing the responsible lifecycle of these batteries, including their disposal and recycling at the end of their operational life, holds considerable importance. These practices not only mitigate the environmental impact but also address the supply vulnerability of battery metals sourced from primary natural reserves. The major compositions of batteries, notably lithium, nickel, and cobalt, are typically concentrated within a material known as black mass, a vital commodity in the battery recycling sector. Solvent extraction is a proficient method for the simultaneous recovery of these materials, to produce a feedstock for new battery manufacturing, aligning with the principles of a circular economy. The separation and purification of these metals, while still challenging, are comparatively less difficult than the requirement posed by primary resource extraction. The co-extraction of multiple compositions in a single step holds tremendous potential for reducing the cost associated with the separation process. Nevertheless, this approach is an unconventional solvent extraction method, necessitating advanced multicomponent extraction models. The present study employed the equilibrium status iteration (ESI) model to describe and predict the extraction performance of battery metals in aqueous solutions. This model is instrumental to the design and optimisation of scaled-up solvent extraction facilities. To illustrate the model, ESI was applied to resolve equilibrium data related to a three-stage counter-current extraction scheme. This removes the laborious trial-and-error phase of experimental inquiry when designing the process involving multiple target metals.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery recycling
Critical minerals
Solvent extraction
Equilibrium modelling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Extraction behaviour of lithium, nickel, manganese, and cobalt with different amount of extractants at O/A of 1, and various equilibrium pH.
pH50, of nickel, cobalt, and manganese at varied extractant volume fractions.
Comparison of organic loading, from ESI prediction and experimental results at equilibrium pH of 8, and various O/A.
Diagram of the three-stage countercurrent solvent extraction unit.
Graphic representation of ESI for lithium concentrations.
Three-stage countercurrent equilibrium results of metals, O/A = 6.

head.ce:title: Multicomponent solvent extraction modelling of lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese from simulated black mass leachate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Parkville, VIC 3010, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Parkville, VIC 3010, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The role of lithium-ion batteries in facilitating the global shift towards renewable energy is paramount. Managing the responsible lifecycle of these batteries, including their disposal and recycling at the end of their operational life, holds considerable importance. These practices not only mitigate the environmental impact but also address the supply vulnerability of battery metals sourced from primary natural reserves. The major compositions of batteries, notably lithium, nickel, and cobalt, are typically concentrated within a material known as black mass, a vital commodity in the battery recycling sector. Solvent extraction is a proficient method for the simultaneous recovery of these materials, to produce a feedstock for new battery manufacturing, aligning with the principles of a circular economy. The separation and purification of these metals, while still challenging, are comparatively less difficult than the requirement posed by primary resource extraction. The co-extraction of multiple compositions in a single step holds tremendous potential for reducing the cost associated with the separation process. Nevertheless, this approach is an unconventional solvent extraction method, necessitating advanced multicomponent extraction models. The present study employed the equilibrium status iteration (ESI) model to describe and predict the extraction performance of battery metals in aqueous solutions. This model is instrumental to the design and optimisation of scaled-up solvent extraction facilities. To illustrate the model, ESI was applied to resolve equilibrium data related to a three-stage counter-current extraction scheme. This removes the laborious trial-and-error phase of experimental inquiry when designing the process involving multiple target metals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global transition to renewable energy hinges on the pivotal role of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as efficient energy storage devices capable of mitigating the disparity between peak electricity demand and renewable energy production from wind or sunlight. However, the current landscape of LIBs has challenges, and one of the primary concerns is the environmental footprint associated with battery manufacturing and disposal processes. LIBs require a substantial volume of critical minerals, as defined by entitles such as the European Union, the United States, and Australia, thereby incurring notable environmental and carbon emission costs. Additionally, the improper disposal of end-of-life LiBs poses issues, like fire and the environmental contamination. Consequently, establishing appropriate disposal methods and recycling technologies are imperative to address both the supply chain vulnerability of key metals within LIBs and the repercussion from discarded LIBs.

The LIB recycling domain is a burgeoning industry with a projected expansion rate that anticipates a market value worth of US$ 7.8 trillion by 2025 [1]. The recycling processes for LiBs bear a semblance to traditional mining operations and can be categorised into process steps including sorting, comminution, shredding, beneficiation, and refining through pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical means. This evolving landscape of LIB recycling has resulted in several interdisciplinary investigations focusing on recycling of packaging materials, collectors, binders, electrolytes, cathode, anodes, and plastic separators of batteries.

Of prominence, hydrometallurgy is distinctive as a key step to recover valuable metals from the black mass generated during recycling. The leaching process is a crucial initial step, liberating metals from solid black mass into an aqueous solution, known as the leachate. Leaching is also an approach to remove undesirable insoluble materials such as graphite and plastic separators. The dissolved metals in the leachate are subsequently subjected to various hydrometallurgical separation technologies, including precipitation, solvent extraction (also known as liquid–liquid extraction), ion exchange, and membrane separation.

The current study centres on solvent extraction, particularly the application of the organophosphorus acid which is predominantly used for recovery of nickel and cobalt from black mass leachate [2]. Increased efficiency can be achieved, avoiding the need to separate and purify each individual metal, by creating streams with mixed elements in the optimal proportions for making new cathodes [3]. This approach, requiring fewer separation stages, has improved cost-effectiveness [4]. The co-extraction of multiple metals offers a promising way to streamline current recycling processes. However, it also necessitates advanced equilibrium models and controlled operating conditions to orchestrate the composition of final products.

Numerical simulations have been deployed to describe the equilibrium of cobalt and nickel extraction using organophosphorus acid, as evidenced by Vasilyev’s work [5]. Similarly, previous study by Lu has described lithium extraction mechanism from mixed solution containing cobalt [6]. Nevertheless, neither approach has holistically captured the equilibrium of co-extracting metals from a solution encompassing lithium, nickel, cobalt, manganese, iron, copper, and aluminium. The complexities associated with actual black mass leachate, with multiple metals pose a great opportunity for further simulation efforts.

The present study extends the modelling framework to forecast the extraction performance of related metals from a simulated black mass leachate. This work offers a thermodynamic model capable of predicting extraction equilibria under diverse operational conditions. In this paper, the thermodynamic model based on previous lithium extraction study [6] is extended to the application of nickel and cobalt extraction process, addressing the modelling gap in the current lithium-ion battery circular economy. The model’s potential lies in streamlining the design of processes aimed to co-extracting metals from black mass leachate. To illustrate the model’s utility, an example is presented, wherein the model facilitates the optimisation of operational conditions and informs the design of a three-stage counter-current solvent extraction unit. This study aims to contribute to the refinement of battery recycling sector and represent a theoretical stride towards sustainable resource management in the realm of lithium-ion battery recycling.

In the lithium-ion battery recycling industry, valuable metals, including cobalt, lithium, and nickel are shredded and collected in a material called black mass, which is a mixed powder of cathode and anode materials [7]. Hydrometallurgy is one of the approaches to recover these metals, which often includes leaching. The concentration of the leachate depends on the spent lithium-ion battery types being recycled, and upstream leaching technology used. This study used a simulated leachate to accommodate most lithium-ion battery types applicable to state-of-art recycling facilities, based on typical black mass compositions and leaching conditions.

The black mass composition used in this study was based on a mixture of cathodes of 20 % LCO (LiCoO2), 10 % NMC523 (LiNi0.5Mn0.2Co0.3O2), 30 % NMC111 (LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2), 20 % NMC811 (LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0. 1O2), 10 % LMO (LiMn2O4) and 10 % LFP (LiFePO4) [8] Upstream leaching was assumed to mainly use hydrochloric acid, one of the most efficient lixiviants [9,10], at optimised temperature, pulp density (volume ratio of solid and liquid), and acidity. The metallic concentrations are proportional to the cathode compositions and other soluble materials from battery packing materials (steel case, aluminium, and copper foils). Cationic elements are present as Li+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Cu2+ after leaching [11], at an oxidation/reduction potential (Eh) of approx. 1000 mV vs. Ag/AgCl [10]. Details of the simulated leachate composition is presented in Table 1 , in comparison with compositions from other literatures.

Both aqueous solution and organic solvent were prepared at room temperature of 21 ± 2 °C, by dissolving/diluting chemicals with RO (reverse osmosis) water or kerosene. Simulated leachate was prepared by dissolving NiCl2·6H2O (>97 %, Chem-Supply), CoCl2·6H2O (>98 %, Aldrich), MnCl2·4H2O (>98 %, Chem-Supply), LiCl (>99 %, Chem-Supply), NaCl (>99.7 %, Chem-Supply), FeCl3·6H2O (>99 %, Sigma-Aldrich), CuCl2·2H2O (>97 %, Chem Supply), AlCl3·6H2O (>95 %, Chem-Supply), hydrochloric acid (Univar) in RO water. The organic solvent, Cyanex 272 (Bis (2,4,4-trimethylpentyl) phosphinic acid) with a density of 0.92 g/mL and molecular weight of 290 g/mol, was supplied by Solvay [14]. TBP (tri-butyl phosphate, >90 %), with a density of 0.980 g/mL, and molecular weight of 266.3 g/mol [15] was from Sigma-Aldrich. Vivasol 2046 (>97 % kerosene), was kindly provided by Viva Energy Australia Pty Ltd [16]. The volume ratio of Cyanex 272 and TBP was controlled at 8:5, to avoid third phase formation during experiments. The organic solvent was saponified using 10 mol/L NaOH dissolved from solid pellets, (>98 % Chem-Supply) if required prior to extraction.

A semicontinuous stirred cell was used for experiments, including a refrigerated/heating circulator (Julabo F12), a jacketed cylinder (Schott Duran, internal volume 250 mL, external volume 600 mL), and a magnetic stirrer (U-lab). A pH meter (Mettler Toledo S213) and a pH probe (Mettler Toledo inlab® Pro-ISM) were used to measure aqueous pH.

Extraction equilibria were conducted at varying pH to investigate the impact of pH on extraction performance. 50 mL of both aqueous solution and organic solvent were mixed in a jacketed cylinder at the room temperature of 21 ± 2 °C. Sodium hydroxide solution was added gradually to adjust pH to the required value. The total volume of sodium hydroxide added was less than 5 % of the original aqueous volume. At each steady state, 0.25 mL of the aqueous phase was pipetted and diluted for analysis. 0.25 mL of the organic solvent was also taken to keep a constant volume ratio of two phases. The experiments were stopped when the pH reached 8.5, as at the beyond value the emulsion formed.

Isotherm experiments were conducted at varying organic to aqueous volume ratios. The solvent was saponified with a ratio of 88.47 mL 10 mol/L NaOH per mol Cyanex 272 prior to experiments, which aimed to control equilibrium pH to 8. The aqueous solution was mixed with varying organic volumes, until equilibrium, before taking 0.25 mL of aqueous samples for analysis.

To measure metallic concentration, aqueous samples were analysed by Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Agilent ICP-720), with additional NaCl to suppress ionisation interference of lithium. Wavelengths at 610.4 nm, 231.6 nm, 293.9 nm, 238.9 nm, 239.6 nm, 324.8 nm, and 396.2 nm were selected for aqueous concentration analysis of Li+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+. While organic concentration

( o r g )
was calculated using mass balance based on the initial aqueous concentration of
c
( M )

, equilibrium metallic concentration of
c

, and organic to aqueous volume ratio (

), using equation (1):
(1)

The organic concentration was also validated by back-extraction. The extraction performance was quantitively measured by the extraction efficiency
E %

The Equilibrium Status Iteration (ESI) model was applied in this study, that was based on a heterogeneous polynuclear complexation process in previous publication [6]. In brief, it assumed the extracting process as a series of heterogeneous complexation reactions. Denoting
NaL
as an organophosphorus acid extractant saponified by NaOH, and
M
n +
as a cationic ion of metals with valence of n. The polynuclear complexation reaction at the interface when using saponified/deprotonated extractant to extract metals from sodium chloride solution is:
(4)
M
a q
n +
+ a
NaL
o r g
+
n
C l
a q
-
⇌
ML
a
n - a
Cl
n - a
o r g
-
+ a
NaCl
a q
Where a is the stoichiometric ratio, as the number of extractant molecules over number of metallic cations in a polynuclear complex
( a q )
or
( o r g )
denotes the species in aqueous or organic phase.

The corresponding heterogeneous equilibrium constant
K 1
in aqueous chloride solution can be calculated by metallic concentration c , and activity coefficient γ of different species:
(5)
K 1

(6)

The values of the activity coefficients of organic and aqueous species were assumed to be constant across the operating range of extraction [6], and therefore:
(7)

where Y is a constant throughout the solvent extraction process in this study.

Thus Equation (5) can be expressed as:
(8)

= Y ∙

c ( N a L )

Including
K 1
and Y in one constant C, called extraction constant as:
(9)
C =
K 1
Y
Expressing in logarithm form as:
(10)
log C = log
D M

c ( N a L )
( o r g )
Values of
log C
and a are specific to individual metal and extractant coordination during extraction. The values of metals in this study were regressed according to experimental results.

In addition, the interface dissociation constant of the organophosphorus acid with abundant aqueous sodium ions was defined as
K
a
′
:
(11)
K
a
′
=
c
( N a L )
( o r g )
∙ c
(
H
+
)
( a q )
c
( H L )
( o r g )
Thus, the aqueous pH in relation to the protonated organophosphorus acid
HL
, and saponified organophosphorus acid
NaL
, is given by:
(12)
pH = - log
K
a
′
- log
HL
o r g
NaL
o r g
The value of
K
a
′
for Cyanex 272 was measured previously as 7.674 × 10−8 mol/L [6]. The influences of modifier (TBP) and diluent (kerosene) were neglected in this study [6].

Regressing of variables a and
log C
is crucial to ESI. Results of these parameters for metals in the simulated leachate are summarised in Table 2 . All parameters were validated across the experimental concentration ranges in this study, specifically 0.0–0.6 mol/L for lithium, and 0.0–0.2 mol/L for nickel, manganese, and cobalt and 0.0–0.1 mol/L for other metals. These parameters were shown to be constant at the tested conditions, which are at a temperature of 21 ± 2 °C and an ionic strength of 1.2 mol/L. Notably, the parameters for manganese and cobalt are very close, with both stoichiometric ratios at around 1.6, and the logarithm of extraction constants approximately at 5.0, indicating a similar complex structure and coordination bonding strength, resulting in similar extraction behaviour with low selectivity. In contrast, lithium’s stoichiometric ratio is approximately 0.7, suggesting a lower amount of organic extractant involved in the extraction. The logarithm of the extraction constant for lithium is 0.13, indicating a weak coordination bonding strength among the extractant and the lithium ions. Additionally, the stoichiometric ratio of nickel is approximately 1.2 and the logarithm of the extraction constant is 2.3, - both values smaller than those of cobalt and manganese. The distinct values of stoichiometric ratio and logarithm of extraction constant represent the unique extraction mechanisms of extractant with different metallic ions, enabling selective recovery of metals.

As presented in Fig. 1 , ESI with regressed parameters described experimental data well. This enabled ESI to predict extraction performance of metals, including lithium, nickel, manganese, cobalt, manganese, copper, iron, and aluminium in this study. In addition, Fig. 1 showed distinct error of ferric ions (see Fig. S1). This was due to the common presence of iron in used RO water, and the concentration of ferric ions in samples was near the ICP-OES detection limit.

The composition of the solvent was optimised to improve the extraction efficiency of targeted metals, including Li, Ni, Mn, Co from simulated leachate. Cyanex 272, was selected as a conventional extractant for Co and Ni separation and also known to extract Li [6]. Extraction performance using different volume fractions of Cyanex 272 was tested and shown in Fig. 2 . Both ESI and experiments implied that Cyanex 272 is an appropriate extractant for all metals from aqueous to organic phase including lithium, and the extraction of metals can be adjusted by pH.

As shown in Fig. 2b, 2c, and 2d, the extraction efficiencies of nickel, manganese, and cobalt, are surpass 99 %. Across all conditions using 12 %, 16 %, 20 % and 24 % Cyanex 272 in the solvent, at pH values above 5–6.5. However, maximum lithium extraction efficiency (a) was lower than 60 %. ESI successfully predicted this extraction equilibria. Lithium was the only element that the extraction efficiency was limited, regardless of the concentration of Cyanex 272. Increasing concentration of Cyanex 272 does not significantly increase the extraction performance of lithium and other metals. 16 v% Cyanex 272 was shown in experiments to enable single stage extracting efficiency to be approx. 100 % for nickel, manganese, and cobalt, as well approx. 50 % for lithium. The yield of lithium can be further improved by increasing the number of extraction stages.

The error of lithium and nickel extraction in Fig. 2 was notable, and the error analysis was conducted as for Fig. S3. Throughout the experiment, the volume fractions of Cyanex 272 deviated from the designed values of 12 %, 16 %, 20 % and 24 %, making it challenging to precisely measure the extractant concentration. The discrepancy in extractant concentration becomes more pronounced at higher pH values, where the deprotonated concentration of Cyanex 272 is greater. This variability could potentially impact the extraction of metals, particularly lithium and nickel at elevated pH values according to Equation (10).

TBP is a common modifier used with Cyanex 272 for solvent extraction to prevent third phase formation [17]. It was shown in preliminary work of this study that ratio of 5 v% TBP to 8 v% Cyanex 272 was sufficient to prevent the third phase formation.

In some studies, TBP was also used with FeCl3 to extract lithium [18,19]. As there are also transition metal chloride salts in leachate, the role of TBP in this study needs to be determined, as it could affect the extraction equilibria if it is also involved in the extraction process as an active extractant. However, preliminary studies indicate that TBP is not involved in extraction as the extraction performances of metals were identical regardless of the additional TBP added (see detail in Text S1, and Fig. S2).

Vivasol 2046, was selected as the diluent and there was no crud or other third phase appearance (such as emulsion, or precipitation) at the optimised extraction conditions. Thus, the impact of different diluents on the extraction equilibrium was regarded minimal in this study [20].

As shown in Fig. 2, adjusting pH is an efficient approach to alter extraction efficiency of different metals. As a common parameter to represent the selectivity, the pH values with 50 % extraction efficiency are defined as pH50 [21]. Results for nickel, cobalt, and manganese with different solvent compositions were compared in Fig. 3 . pH50 generally decreases at higher extractant concentration, implying that higher concentration of extractant is beneficial to extract metals at a low pH range. The difference of pH50 for different metals, shown in Fig. 3, presents the selectivity of different metals. As Cyanex 272 is a commercialised solvent for cobalt and nickel separation, differences of cobalt and nickel’s pH50 values are larger than 1.3, implying a good separation between cobalt and nickel. In contrast, difference of cobalt and manganese’s pH50 values using Cyanex 272 is approx. 0.3, suggesting difficulty separating cobalt and manganese.

Regarding the extraction performance of simulated leachate used in this study, ESI was applied to predict the performance, and was compared with experimental isotherm results at varying O/A ratios. Fig. 4 indicates ESI prediction can be validated by experimental results. Compared to other metals, lithium organic loading is more sensitive to operating conditions. Besides, the prediction of lithium deviates from the experimental value (as shown in both Fig. 4, and Fig. S4), and this is attributed to the deviation observed in the predicted distribution ratio at pH of 8, as identified in Fig. 2(a). Note results of ferric ions are not shown, as at the tested pH of 8, soluble ferric ions were under the detection range of ICP-OES. To conclude, ESI was capable of predict equilibria of organic and aqueous phases species, which are paramount for operating condition optimisation and process design.

Previous experiments indicated the potential use of Cyanex 272 to co-extract valuable metals from the simulated leachate. Here, ESI is applied in aiding the design of a three-stage countercurrent extraction. As shown in Fig. 5 , Lean organic solvent and the leachate enter the unit counter currently. The metallic ion loading in the organic phase increases along the cascade. The calculation shows that, with an O/A ratio of 6, a three-stage counter extraction design can achieve the overall yield of lithium as high as 99.0 %. Fig. 5 presents concentrations of organic and aqueous flows from each stage, with metallic concentrations denoted as
c [
M
o r g
n +
]
i
and
c [

a q
n +
]
i
respectively in organic and aqueous phases. Wherein i denotes the stage number from which flows originate. The aqueous leachate enters the first stage, while the aqueous raffinate leaving the third stage is labelled as
c [
M
a q
n +
]
out
. The organic flow existing the first stage is the loaded solvent, indicated as
c [
M
o r g
n +
]
out
, and the organic flow entering the third stage is the lean solvent from the saponification step.

A mass balance for stage i , with rearrangement, yields Equation (13)
(13)
A O

For a three-stage countercurrent extraction, the overall mass balance can also be calculated as follows:
(14)
A O

Typically, a countercurrent extraction scheme may use a McCabe-Thiele diagram to graphically determine the number of stages based on an operating line and an equilibrium line. In this work, the equilibrium lines of metals are not fixed, but instead require iterative calculations for each stage, depending on the compositions of the aqueous and organic phases from previous stages [21]. This iterative equilibrium calculation involves the simultaneous considering of all metallic species, using ESI. The crucial parameter in this iterative process is the concentration of deprotonated Cyanex 272, denoted as
c
(
L
-
)
( o r g )
, at each equilibrium stage, which dictates the metal distribution ratios (
D M
) for each stage. The iteration also includes the overall mass balance of Cyanex 272 and necessitates convergence at each stage.

The graphical representation of lithium concentration, exemplified in Fig. 6 in a McCabe-Thiele style, omits the equilibrium line as it was determined from the simultaneous iteration of multiple species at each stage and cannot be depicted as a fixed line in the diagram. For lithium, with a selected O/A ratio of 6, the initial lithium concentration in the feed, as listed in Table 1, is 0.222 mol/L, and initial concentrations in the aqueous raffinate and the lean solvent were assumed to be 0 for the first iteration. Therefore, the lithium concentration in the loaded solvent was calculated as 0.037 mol/L, which can also be determined by drawing a vertical line from the aqueous feed composition to find the intersection point (point A) with the operating line in Fig. 6.

Next, the multi-species equilibrium was calculated for the first stage using ESI. After convergence, the concentration
c
(
L
-
)
( o r g )
on the first stage was 0.338 mol/L, leading to a lithium distribution ratio of 0.646, as per Equation (10), and an aqueous lithium concentration of 0.057 mol/L following Equation (6). This is also represented graphically in Fig. 6, by point B
(
c [
Li
a q
+
]
1
,
c [
Li
o r g
+
]
1
)
.

For the second stage, the organic lithium concentration, as determined by Equation (13), was
c [
Li
o r g
+
]
2
= 0.095 mol/L. ESI model was again employed to calculate the equilibrium point for the second stage, resulting in
c [
Li
a q
+
]
2
= 0.012 mol/L.

As the cascade progresses to the third stage, the raffinate’s lithium concentration was calculated as
c [
Li
a q
+
]
3
= 0.003 mol/L using ESI and mass balance. This deviated from the initial assumption of lithium concentration in the raffinate of 0, needing a second iteration. The acceptable error used in this calculation was less than 0.4 % for lithium and less than 0.01 % for other metals in this study. The results are summarised in Table 3 . Note the composition of lean solvent was determined through ESI during the stripping and saponification steps, with specific conditions applied for these steps, including an equilibrium pH of 0.3 and the O/A ratio of 10 for stripping, and an equilibrium pH of 8 and the O/A ratio of 6 for saponification.

While the yield of lithium showed an apparent increase, the extraction of other metals exhibited high yield at the first stage. The second and third stages, however, primarily focused on the recovery of lithium. The ability to predict and potentially fine-tune the process to target recovery of specific metals demonstrated the importance of a multicomponent equilibrium modelling when scaling up, which is crucial for optimising the simultaneous extraction of multiple metals from a complex leachate solution.

This study applied a thermodynamic model (ESI) to predict the multicomponent extraction equilibrium of seven metals, including lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese, using Cyanex 272. The experiments were conducted with a simulated solution to capture the potential black mass leachate in the lithium-ion battery recycling sector. The experimental validation demonstrated ESI’s capability to predict the extraction performance across varied operational parameters, including pH variation, extractant compositions, and organic-to-aqueous phase ratios. Using ESI, a three-stage counter-current extraction system could be engineered to enhance overall lithium yield.

However, the ESI was restricted to the operating conditions tested in this study, deliberately excluding nonideal phenomena like precipitation and gelation. Further exploration of the organic behaviour may also hold the key insight to resolving issues related to crud or gelation formation when the system operates under conditions beyond the scope of this study.

Crucially, the black mass from the lithium-ion battery recycling sector exhibits varying concentrations and metal species, dependent on upstream collection practices. Consequently, the solvent extraction process to recover valuable metals are often vulnerable to feed variation, necessitating frequent operational adjustment. The approach demonstrated in this study, offers a straightforward model capable of predicting extraction performance during bench-scale and scale-up operations. This capability translates to labour and time savings, and facilitate the process design phase, enabling rapid adaptation of solvent extraction to the dynamic demands of practical black mass recycling technology.

Junnan Lu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wen Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Geoff W. Stevens: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology. Kathryn A. Mumford: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the financial support provided by the Melbourne Research Scholarship and the Clive Pratt Scholarship during the investigation.
